variables.content: In diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) simulations of the positron-in-HEG system performed with a fixed-node constraint and a discrete time step Δt, the proposition is that extrapolating computed DMC energies to zero time step using calculations at multiple finite Δt values removes the leading-order time-step bias so that the reported extrapolated DMC energies accurately approximate the continuous-time (Δt→0) DMC limit within the stated uncertainties.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

Consider the reported DMC calculations carried out with finite DMC time steps (time steps of 0.04, 0.2, and 0.4 a.u. at \\(r_{s}=1,5,10\\) for paramagnetic HEGs and 0.01, 0.2, and 0.4 a.u. at \\(r_{s}=1,5,10\\) for ferromagnetic HEGs) and using single-determinant Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions that, according to extrapolation of variational Monte Carlo energies to zero energy variance, retrieve more than 99% of the correlation energy. The proposition is: the residual biases arising from the fixed-node approximation and from finite DMC time-step errors (noting that halving the time step was verified to have a negligible effect on the computed energy band) are small compared with the overall uncertainties introduced by the finite-size extrapolation procedure; therefore these residual numerical biases do not materially shift the reported extrapolated thermodynamic-limit effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\).

For the methane dimer the study reports fixed-node DMC interaction energies at two time steps, $0.05\ \text{a.u.}$ and $0.01\ \text{a.u.}$, and uses the comparison between these two points to assess finite-time-step bias; no systematic extrapolation of DMC energies to the zero-time-step limit was presented, so residual finite-time-step bias may remain.
      Figure 3

Let DMC calculations with the fixed-node approximation be carried out for ground and single-particle excited states of a 2D HEG using single-determinant Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions (real determinants for closed-shell ground states, Jastrow factors with polynomial and plane-wave terms, and polynomial backflow functions). The proposition is: the residual fixed-node errors introduced by the nodal surface of these single-determinant Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions are sufficiently small and similar in magnitude for the ground and corresponding single-particle excited states that the fixed-node error contributions to the total-energy differences that define the single-quasiparticle band \\(\\mathcal{E}(\\mathbf{k})\\) are negligible compared with the statistical uncertainties and the finite-size effects analysed; hence fixed-node bias does not materially alter the excitation-energy trends reported within the stated numerical precision.

Diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) propagates an importance-sampled imaginary-time Schrödinger equation with a discrete time step τ and yields an estimate E_DMC(τ) that approaches the fixed-node continuum limit E_DMC(0) as τ → 0; the finite-time-step bias δE(τ) ≡ E_DMC(τ) − E_DMC(0) can be non-negligible for practical choices of τ, and for time steps of order τ = 0.125 H^−1 and τ = 0.10 H^−1 (where H^−1 denotes inverse Hartree atomic units) the systematic time-step error can scale approximately as a τ + b τ^2 depending on implementation details, so that if reported DMC energies are obtained at such finite τ without an explicit τ → 0 extrapolation or without demonstrating that δE(τ) ≪ the reported per-dimer energy differences (for example, ≪ 0.02 eV), then the finite-τ bias could be comparable to the small energy differences used to infer energetic preferences.

Let τ denote the DMC time step in atomic units and let E_DMC(τ;N) denote the fixed-node DMC energy computed for a cluster of size N at time step τ; the proposition is that selecting a single DMC time step τ = 0.01 au produces a time-step bias ΔE_τ(N) ≡ E_DMC(0.01;N) - lim_{τ→0} E_DMC(τ;N) that is negligible for all cluster sizes considered, specifically that |ΔE_τ(N)| ≪ 1 kJ mol⁻¹ so that no explicit time-step extrapolation beyond the single chosen value was required to obtain chemically accurate energies across N.

In fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (FN-DMC), let $E_{\text{DMC}}(\tau)$ denote the FN-DMC energy estimated with a short time step $\tau$ (in atomic units). For sufficiently small $\tau$ where the leading time-step bias is approximately linear in $\tau$, performing a linear extrapolation of $E_{\text{DMC}}(\tau)$ to $\tau\to 0$ using two small time steps $\tau_1=2\times 10^{-4}$ and $\tau_2=4\times 10^{-4}$ yields an estimate of the zero-time-step FN-DMC energy via the formula $E_{\text{DMC}}(0)=E_{\text{DMC}}(\tau_1)-\dfrac{E_{\text{DMC}}(\tau_2)-E_{\text{DMC}}(\tau_1)}{\tau_2-\tau_1}\,\tau_1$, within the quoted statistical uncertainties in the present calculations.

In fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) the discrete imaginary-time step dτ used for walker propagation produces Trotter (time-step) errors that can materially alter computed quasiparticle energy bands and hence the derivative-based effective mass m*: in the authors' calculations for paramagnetic 2D-UEL at r_s = 5 with N = 302 electrons, the DMC band fits obtained using dτ = 0.005 a.u. and dτ = 0.2 a.u. (each run using the same walker ensemble size N_w = 2560 and the same number of propagation steps 25×10^3) exhibit markedly different band-fitting uncertainties and produce differing estimates of m*, demonstrating that insufficient time-step control can bias m* and that the common assumption that time-step errors cancel in excitation-energy differences need not hold.

For weak intermolecular interaction energies whose magnitudes are of order $10^{-4}\ \text{Hartree}$ (sub-chemical accuracy), the fixed-node DMC calculations reported here indicate that a DMC time step on the order of $0.01\ \text{atomic units}$ or smaller is required to reduce finite-time-step bias to below the interaction-energy magnitude; larger time steps such as $0.05\ \text{a.u.}$ can leave a residual time-step bias comparable to the interaction-energy values.
